title: SoftMatcha 2: A Fast and Soft Pattern Matcher for Trillion-Scale Corpora
abstract: We present SoftMatcha 2, an ultra-fast and flexible search algorithm that enables search over trillion-scale natural language corpora in under 0.3 seconds while allowing semantic variations in the form of substitution, insertion, and deletion. Our approach employs string matching based on suffix arrays that scales well with corpus size, and represents words as vectors, which underpin its semantic flexibility. To mitigate the combinatorial explosion induced by the semantic relaxation of queries, our method is built on two key algorithmic ideas: dynamic corpus-aware pruning and fast exact lookup enabled by a disk-aware design. We theoretically analyze the efficiency of the proposed method, indicating that it can mitigate exponential growth in the search space. Empirically, on FineWeb-Edu (Lozhkov et al., 2024) (1.4T tokens), it attains substantially lower search latency than existing methods: infini-gram (Liu et al., 2024), infini-gram mini (Xu et al., 2025), and SoftMatcha (Deguchi et al., 2025). As a practical application, our method uncovers benchmark contamination in training corpora that existing approaches miss, and it also benefits information retrieval and paraphrase detection. We also provide an online demo of fast, soft search across corpora in seven languages.
decision_comment:
This paper proposes a fast, soft search engine designed for trillion-scale text corpora. The contribution is to maintain the scalability of suffix-array-style exact lookups while extending the functionality to semantic matching, which allows for word substitution, insertion, and deletion. To achieve this, the authors combine a disk-aware staged suffix array with dynamic corpus-aware pruning. As noted by the reviewers, the pruning is very important (as is the case in all 'search' indices).  Rather than exploding over all relaxed query variants, the method incrementally filters candidates based on whether similar prefixes actually occur in the corpus.

The reviewers and I agree that the paper offers a very clear systems-level contribution by making "soft" phrase-level search practical at the trillion-token scale. The method demonstrates sub-second latency for soft searches, which is practically impressive. Additionally, the paper successfully demonstrates a downstream use case in benchmark contamination detection. Reviewers also appreciated that the paper provides a theoretical "story" (under many assumptions which seem to be practically motivated). for an intuition behind the method.

However, there are several weaknesses that the reviewers and I have noted: First, it is not entirely clear to me what the exact formal problem setting is, because the problem definition and the experimental setting seem to be intertwined and introduced together. Some reviewers cited underlying problems of organization, self-containedness, and technical exposition. Relatedly, while the method has very strong empirical runtime results, the presentation could be better formally specified, and  the theory seems only loosely connected to the broader claims. Maybe most importantly, the paper does not discuss (at a technical level) nearest neighbor search indices that solve a related problem of retrieving the closest document in similarity, such as DiskANN, HNSW, or IVF. Even though these methods retrieve without the soft min condition over individual words, they are quite relevant and must be discussed.

The paper also seems to optimize for speed, but much less so on retrieval quality. There are also tradeoffs on memory, and some settings require up to 21.6TB. This seems to be a real bottleneck in practical deployment. 

The authors also provided some preliminary LLM-as-a-judge and BM25 quantitative evaluations in the rebuttal which strengthen the experiments. However, a more systematic evaluation of search quality is not present. Despite the significant presentation issues and the need for broader evaluation, the strong runtime results for trillion-scale soft search seem valuable to the community. Given this, I believe a weak accept is appropriate for the paper.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a fast soft search engine for trillion-scale text corpora. The core idea is to keep the scalability of suffix-array-style exact lookup, but extend it to semantic matching with substitution, insertion, and deletion by combining a disk-aware staged suffix array with dynamic corpus-aware pruning. The pruning is the key trick: instead of exploding over all relaxed query variants, it incrementally filters candidates using whether similar prefixes actually occur in the corpus. On FineWeb-Edu at 1.4T tokens, they report 0.34 ms p95 exact lookup and 278 ms p95 soft search, substantially faster than infini-gram, infini-gram mini, and SoftMatcha at large scale, while also supporting softer matching that the exact methods cannot do. They also show a practical use case in benchmark contamination detection, where soft search finds additional contamination cases missed by exact matching, including semantic variants and templated numeric substitutions.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

1. The paper has a very clear systems contribution. It is not trying to learn a new retriever; it is making soft phrase-level search actually practical at trillion-token scale, which is a real gap in current corpus search tools.

2. The core algorithmic idea is pretty nice. Combining a staged suffix-array design with dynamic corpus-aware pruning is a clean way to keep the scalability of exact lookup while avoiding the usual combinatorial blowup of relaxed search.

3. I like that the paper is not just heuristic. It gives a theoretical story for why pruning should tame the search space under natural-language statistics, which makes the method feel more principled than “we tried some pruning and it worked.”

4. Running soft search on FineWeb-Edu at 1.4T tokens with sub-second p95 latency is a meaningful result, and the exact-search latency is also very strong.

5. The paper shows a concrete user case--contamination detection.

Weakness

1. The contamination application is a relatively simple user case where the targeted documents is defined to be strictly similar to the query. The application to tasks where more implicit semantic similarity or reasoning-intensive search might not benefit much from this method.

2. The deployment cost is still pretty heavy -- 21.6TB is a lot for many people to afford using it.

3. The precision of contamination detection is 81%, which is not perfectly precise.

4. The latency might not be as ideal as in the paper's setup when the user wants to take a larger K or lower threshold?
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Right now the paper is very convincing on speed, but much less so on retrieval quality. Can you provide a more systematic search-quality evaluation beyond the contamination case study?

2. How much of the practical gain depends on the specific operating point (similarity threshold, top-K, query length)?

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the soft (semantic) pattern matching on a trillion scale corpus. The similarity of a query and a candidate text span is measured by some soft version of minimum cosine distance between individual word embeddings. The method proposed is based on suffix array with two key algorithmic ideas: fast exact lookup enabled by a disk-aware design, and dynamic corpus-aware pruning. Theorectical results were provided, exploiting the Zipf distribution typical in natural language text corpus. Experimental results show that the proposed method obtains speed up up to 33x.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
S1: Nice original engineering achievement with impressive performance speedup. 

S2: The pruning is based on a clever observation of the Zipf law, with clear justification (Sec 4), resulting in the sublinear scaling results. 

S3: The paper evaluated 6 languages with many metrics, demonstrating the broader application of this method.  

W1: The method works at the word level, hence cannot deal with compound words or phrases (e.g., Hong Kong). In addition, the use of suffix array means the method is sensitive to word/phrase ordering, which may arise frequently in practice. Further more, "importance of the machine learning" may not match "significance of machine learning algorithms" do the large norm of "algorithms". 

As such, the proposed method is better described as lexical-semantic matching, rather than semantic matching. 

W2: The theoretical derivation hinges upon Hypothesis 1, which was testable but not done. This weakens the validity of Thms 1 and 2. Specifically, the Hypothesis assumes query-similar patterns have the same corpus occurrence rate as random patterns. But query-similar patterns are semantically coherent and thus more likely to occur than random 𝑖-grams.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Precisions are reported in the contamination experiments, what about recalls?
2. Ablation studies are need for some of the important hyper-parameters, such as 𝛽
3. Which algorithm did you use in Faiss? IVF or HNSW?
reviews.final_justification:
I thank the authors for the constructive rebuttal. The additional experiments address my main concerns and strengthen the paper’s soundness.

The work is original and practically useful, with strong system-level contributions. However, it is better viewed as lexical-semantic matching, with some limitations in compositional semantics, which somewhat limits its broader impact.

Overall, the rebuttal reinforces my prior assessment. I maintain my weak accept recommendation and encourage clearer scope definition in the final version.

reviews.summary: The paper develops a search algorithm for exact and "soft" queries over huge ordered text corpora. A query is defined as an ordered sequence of tokens/words. Soft queries are \emph{semantic} variation of the original query, with substituted, inserted, or deleted tokens. These "patterns" are generated by enumerating possible variations of the original query that may appear in the dataset. The proposed algorithm has two main components: (1) a fast disk-based exact lookup procedure built on suffix arrays, and (2) a dynamic pruning procedure that iteratively removes candidate patterns using corpus-occurrence information obtained during search. The paper provides an informal theoretical analysis, along with a numeric evaluation against benchmark methods, with particular emphasis on a very large corpus containing 1.4 trillion tokens. The paper also presents a contamination-detection application showing the benefit of soft queries by identifying additional near-matching benchmark sequences beyond what exact-matching algorithms can find.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper addresses a timely and important problem. Search algorithms are relevant to a number of growing applications, such as training-data auditing, contamination analysis, and investigating model behavior. The problem is especially important as corpora continue to grow in size, having reached trillion-token scales and expanding. The numerical results show a clear advantage for the proposed algorithm over extant methods, particularly in online search speed.

Despite these strengths, the paper contains major weaknesses that undermine its proposed contributions and novelty, which must be addressed before being considered for publication. Below is a list of those weaknesses in no particular order of importance.

1. Evaluation and Rigor

A main weakness of the paper is related to its biggest claimed strength. The numerical results show significant gains over prior methods, but they do not tell the full story. First, the millisecond results in Figures 3, 4, and 8 refer to isolated \emph{online} queries over a heavily preprocessed index and do not reflect end-to-end search cost. The proposed system requires substantial offline indexing, and while the paper does report index construction time and disk usage, these costs are not grounded proportionally to their practical importance.

The proposed algorithm does show favorable offline preprocessing time relative to some baselines, but this comes with a very large storage footprint. More importantly, the paper does not sufficiently disentangle algorithmic improvement from the underlying time–memory tradeoff. While the proposed system is faster online, it also relies on substantial offline indexing and a very large disk-resident structure. The paper should be much more explicit about this tradeoff and justify why it is favorable in realistic deployments.

The paper also notes that some competing methods, such as SoftMatcha, exceed RAM limits at the largest scales considered. However, it remains unclear whether the proposed method is genuinely less memory-intensive overall, or whether it primarily shifts resource requirements from RAM to disk. The evaluation does not provide a clean RAM vs disk vs total-memory comparison across methods, which makes it difficult to assess practical efficiency.

Second, the empirical evaluation remains too narrow. The paper primarily emphasizes latency-based metrics, especially p95, while omitting a fuller runtime profile such as mean latency and clearer reporting of peak RAM usage and total memory footprint. More importantly, for a paper with a main contribution of semantic soft search, it does not provide a broad quantitative evaluation of retrieval quality or relevance. Prior work such as SoftMatcha reports effectiveness-oriented metrics, whereas this paper largely limits itself to runtime, storage, and qualitative examples.

2. Presentation and Motivation

The paper is poorly structured and not sufficiently self-contained. The introduction begins with a broad and lengthy discussion of recent progress in Large Language Models (LLM) and eventually arrives at search-related applications. It then spends considerable space emphasizing the growth of corpus size, but without clearly articulating the technical challenges this creates for the problem at hand. The paper eventually states what its algorithm does, but it does not sufficiently unpack the underlying bottlenecks, the failure modes of existing approaches, or why its particular combination of techniques is the right response. The more concrete challenge statement appears only later in Section 3, where the paper identifies the two main challenges as slow naive suffix-array lookup and exponential candidate growth. This makes the introduction feel under-motivated relative to what the method actually hinges on.

The related-work sections (2.1 and 2.2) are mostly framing and positioning. The paper spends substantial space on broad background in corpus linguistics, string matching, information retrieval, and dense retrieval before arriving at the most direct comparisons in Section 2.3. As a result, these sections read mostly like an extended introduction rather than a sharply comparative related-work discussion. Even in Section 2.3, where the most relevant baselines are discussed, the paper often states high-level distinctions rather than carefully isolating what is genuinely new algorithmically.

Most importantly, the main body is a high-level description of the method, and many core choices are presented more as engineering decisions than as fully justified technical responses to the problem. The main algorithm is explicitly labeled "simplified", the staged suffix array is deferred to Appendix H, the additional pruning techniques to Appendix I, and the theory details to Appendix J. Thus, while the main body introduces the claimed contributions, many of the details needed to understand or assess them are deferred to the appendices. Given the page limit, it would have been more appropriate to devote more of the main paper to the proposed technical contributions rather than to broad background material.

The paper also assumes too much prior knowledge from the reader. The full setup should be defined more explicitly and self-contained in the main body. Instead, many concepts are introduced with only brief citation-level justification. An expert reader can perhaps follow the intended meaning, but the burden is clearly placed on the reader.

Lastly, the notation is introduced too loosely. Several symbols appear without clear definition, forcing the reader to infer their meaning from context. For example, $\mathcal{V}$ is used for the first time on page 5, Section 4, but never defined. The paper would benefit from a more rigorous and self-contained presentation of the problem setting and notation.

3. Disconnect Between Motivated Contributions and Actual Contributions.

There is a substantial disconnect between what the paper motivates and what it actually establishes through theory and experiments. For example, at the end of page 1 the paper identifies three "unique needs of modern corpus searching" and then states, "We propose a fast full-text search algorithm that meets the three desiderata (1–3) mentioned above". However, only the first point, namely scale, is directly and convincingly supported by the paper’s results. The other two are asserted as motivation, but not meaningfully established by the theory or experiments. In particular, the paper does not directly show that the method improves LLM auditing workflows, and the motivation around non-deterministic training and next-token prediction is stated much more strongly than it is demonstrated. These claims should therefore either be substantially toned down or supported with direct evidence. The paper would be stronger if it focused more narrowly on what it actually addresses, rather than appealing to broader connections that it does not substantiate.

Conversely, the paper’s main technical contributions are not well motivated in the introduction, nor are they sufficiently developed through the related-work discussion in Section 2. For example, why is a suffix-array-based approach more appropriate here? Why is enumerating candidate patterns and pruning them the right design choice? Why are these challenges difficult, and why are existing methods insufficient? What is the role of the disk-aware staged suffix array, and why does it address the problem better than a more modest extension (for example, by moving from RAM to disk) of prior work? The paper later achieves a large run-time disk compression that is never mentioned in the introduction. Similarly, the advantage of soft search vs exact search (in finding more results) is numerically showed but not sufficiently motivated. Even the summarized contributions stated at the end of the introduction are not discussed prior to their summary!

These are central questions for understanding the paper’s actual contribution.

4. Novelty and Contributions

The paper’s novelty appears to lie primarily in a nontrivial engineering combination of existing ideas, rather than in a fundamentally new algorithmic principle. In particular, the proposed fast exact lookup component seems to rely heavily on the fact that the target queries are short: Appendix H states that the method exploits short queries, and uses $L=12$ in the experiments. The resulting two-stage lookup strategy stores a sampled index in RAM, uses it to identify a small candidate block, and then searches only that block on disk. The paper itself notes that this design is similar to Google's BigTable. Thus, the contribution is more naturally framed as an external-memory engineering procedure tailored to trillion-token, short-query corpus search rather than a fundamentally new "disk-aware" approach as motivated.

The proposed method also enumerates query-similar candidate patterns, repeatedly prunes them, and verifies survivors through exact lookup. This makes the effectiveness of pruning central to the method’s practicality, and raises a potential limitation when pruning is less effective than in the corpora studied here. The paper argues that this combinatorial growth is mitigated, but the justification is only partial.

The clearest demonstrated contribution is faster online search over heavily preprocessed data. Although the paper also includes a contamination-detection application, it remains unclear how much this speedup changes a realistic downstream workflow, for example in LLM auditing (as the paper motivates). In that sense, the practical contribution is not fully clear beyond improved online speed. The contamination contribution shows usefulness, but not enough to rescue the broader novelty claim.

Many of the paper’s design choices are only stated, and the reasoning behind those choices is not well articulated. This seems ad-hoc. For example, the paper introduces a modified similarity function and states that it improves over the plain minimum, but provides little analytical or empirical justification for that claim. Similarly, choices such as $m=5$ or $L=12$, and the adaptive-thresholding target $K$ are introduced with limited justification.  

The theoretical analysis in Section 4 is very weak. Hypothesis 1 is more of an assumption than a validated hypothesis, and the paper justifies it only by stating that it is believed to hold "from a linguistic perspective". The resulting theory is therefore built on author-introduced assumptions rather than on established properties of the model or corpus. Moreover, the main results are explicitly presented as informal theorems, and the second theorem additionally relies on a Zipf-law assumption for n-gram frequencies. The main sublinearity claim is therefore conditional on multiple strong assumptions. 

There also appears to be a mismatch between the theory and the implemented system. The theory analyzes the average number of exact lookups in the simplified Algorithm 1 under fixed thresholds, whereas the practical system uses adaptive thresholding and additional heuristics. As a result, it is unclear how directly the empirical scaling plots correspond to the theoretical claims. The non-monotonic behavior in Figure 8 may not be inconsistent with the stated theory, but it is left unexplained. Taken together, these issues substantially weaken the paper’s claims to novelty, rigor, and well-justified contribution


Minor comments:

The English queries were generated and adjusted using Gemini 3.0 Pro rather than drawn from a natural workload. That weakens the external validity of the latency evaluation.

The appendix explicitly says cache was not cleared after each query, so later queries may benefit from retained cached data. That matters for interpreting the latency numbers, particularly in the millisecond scale.

The paper is really evaluating a heavily pre-indexed deployed system, not raw corpus search. That may be worth stating explicitly once.

The paper claims to "theoretically show" mitigation of exponential growth, which is stronger than what Section 4 justifies. That mismatch is worth pointing out explicitly.

The paper does not clearly separate what is novel relative to the literature (such as SoftMatcha) versus what is novel relative to external-memory indexing ideas more broadly.
reviews.limitations: The paper uses the generic impact statement. See previous section for limitations and suggestions for improvement.
reviews.final_justification: My main concerns remain only partially resolved. The added paraphrase-style retrieval result is a useful step toward evaluating semantic search quality, but it is still relatively limited, and I still do not see a broad quantitative effectiveness evaluation that supports the paper’s soft-search claims. Similarly, the theory still appears fundamentally assumption-driven and conditional. The hypothesis is an assumption that the authors are reluctant to frame as such. Finally, the proposed writing and structural revisions sound promising, but cannot be sufficiently evaluated at the current stage.
